1.exposition: The vision that opened with the dragon (ch. 12) now shows the dragon's first agent. A monstrous beast rises out of the sea — seven heads, ten horns, ten diadems, blasphemous names — a deliberate composite of the four beasts of Daniel 7, gathering every empire's ferocity into one. The dragon hands it his own power, throne, and authority. One of its heads is wounded to death, yet the death-stroke is healed, and the astonished world worships both dragon and beast, crying, 'Who is like the beast?' For forty-two months it blasphemes God and is permitted to make war on the saints and conquer them, wielding authority over every tribe and nation. All the earth worships it — all, that is, but those whose names stand in the Lamb's book of life. The section ends not in panic but in a summons: here is the endurance and faith of the saints.
1.original_language: The creature is a thērion (a wild, predatory 'beast,' not the neutral zōon of the throne-room living creatures). Its body is stitched together from Daniel's leopard, bear, and lion — though John reverses Daniel's order, as if the eye pans from the nearest empire back toward Babylon. The pivotal phrase is the wounded head 'as though slain': esphagmenēn, the very participle used of the Lamb 'as though slain'. The beast's healed death-stroke (hē plēgē tou thanatou) is a studied counterfeit of the Lamb's death and resurrection, and the world 'marveled' (ethaumasthē) after it — the wonder owed to God redirected to his ape.
1.historical_context: For John's first readers the beast wore the face of Rome. The sea is the Mediterranean out of which imperial power reached Asia, and 'the sea' is elsewhere the restless mass of peoples and nations. The healed mortal wound most naturally evoked the Nero redivivus legend — the widespread first-century belief that Nero, dead in 68, would return from the east at the head of Parthian armies — which the church saw re-embodied in Domitian's Nero-like demand for worship. 'Who is like the beast?' (v.4) is a blasphemous inversion of Israel's confession, 'Who is like you, O LORD?' (Exodus 15:11) — the very meaning the name Michael declares (12:7). The imperial cult, with its temples and festivals across the province, pressed exactly this question of worship.
1.christ: The beast is an anti-Lamb. It receives its power, throne, and authority from the dragon just as the Son receives all things from the Father; it bears a slain-and-healed head as the Lamb bears his slain-and-risen life; it gathers the worship of 'every tribe and people and language and nation' — the very humanity the Lamb redeemed by his blood. Against this towering parody John sets one quiet phrase: the Lamb's book of life, the roll of the redeemed written before the foundation of the world. The beast's dominion is loud, universal, and temporary; the Lamb's register is silent, particular, and eternal — and it, not the beast, has the last word on who belongs to whom.

verses.note: John sees a beast rising out of the sea, with seven heads and ten horns, ten diadems on the horns and a blasphemous name on the heads. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill identify it with the Roman power — the fourth beast of Daniel 7 — arising from the 'sea' of tumultuous nations (17:15); the crowned horns mark its royal dominion, and the name marks the divine honors it arrogates. The latin-fathers Victorinus and Bede read the kingdom of Antichrist. The identity is a genuine crux — imperial (and later papal) Rome to historicists, Nero's Rome to preterists, a coming Antichrist-kingdom to futurists — yet all agree on the substance: a blasphemous world-power risen from the nations.

The beast is like a leopard, with a bear's feet and a lion's mouth, and 'the dragon gave him his power and his throne and great authority.' grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that it fuses the first three beasts of Daniel 7 — Greece, Persia, Babylon — concentrating every empire's swiftness, strength, and devouring cruelty into one comprehensive persecutor. Its authority is not its own: it is the visible instrument of the dragon, energized and enthroned by Satan himself.

One of its heads seemed wounded to death, but the mortal wound was healed, and the whole world followed the beast in wonder. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson see a persecuting power struck an apparently fatal blow and then reviving; reformed Gill reads the empire wounded and rising again in a new form. Others hear the Nero redivivus expectation. Whatever the referent, the healed death-stroke is a parody of resurrection — the counterfeit by which the beast wins the world's astonished allegiance.

The world worships the dragon and the beast, crying, 'Who is like the beast, and who can fight against it?' grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill observe that to worship the beast is, in effect, to worship the dragon who empowers it — satanic idolatry. The acclamation is a blasphemous parody of Israel's praise, 'Who is like you, O LORD?' (Exodus 15:11) — the confession the name Michael itself declares (12:7). Brute, seemingly invincible power is deified.

The beast is given a mouth uttering great things and blasphemies, and authority for forty-two months. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson trace the arrogant mouth to the little horn of Daniel 7, which spoke great words against the Most High (Daniel 7:25). The forty-two months — three and a half years, the same broken period as 11:2 and 12:6 — mark a reign that is real but strictly bounded; the repeated 'was given' insists the beast rages only by God's permission.

The beast opens its mouth to blaspheme God's name, his dwelling, and those who dwell in heaven. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott read a threefold assault: God's revealed character ('his name'), his tabernacle — taken as heaven, or as the church in which God dwells by his Spirit — and the heavenly host of angels and glorified saints. The blasphemy is comprehensive, reaching to everything holy.

It is allowed to make war on the saints and to conquer them, with authority over every tribe and people and tongue and nation. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson hear the little horn of Daniel 7, which warred on the saints and prevailed; the conquest is outward and bodily, for the appointed time, even as the martyrs conquer in faith. Its universal sway is a dark echo of the Lamb's redeemed from every tribe and nation — yet 'was given' again bounds it.

All who dwell on earth will worship it — all whose names are not written in the Lamb's book of life. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott mark the saving exception: the elect, kept from the idolatry, are distinguished not by their strength but by their enrollment. reformed Gill grounds that enrollment before the foundation of the world, the chosen written in Christ (Ephesians 1:4); eastern Cyril refers 'the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world' to God's eternal purpose, in which Christ's sacrifice was resolved before time. The book of life is the beast's true rival.

'If anyone has an ear, let him hear.' grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson recognize the formula of the seven letters (2:7) and of Christ himself (Matthew 11:15). Standing here, apart from any promise, it arrests the reader before the weighty word that follows about captivity, the sword, and the endurance of the saints.

'If anyone is to be taken captive, to captivity he goes; if anyone is to be slain by the sword, by the sword he must be slain.' grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson read the law of just retribution — the persecutor repaid in kind (Matthew 26:52; Genesis 9:6); grammatical-historical Ellicott hears in it comfort for the persecuted, whose oppressor's violence will recoil on itself. 'Here is the endurance and faith of the saints' — the point is not vengeance but the patient, believing endurance this certainty makes possible.

witnesses.point: The sea-beast is the Roman power — Daniel's fourth beast risen from the tumult of the nations — its crowned horns and blasphemous name marking a dominion that arrogates divine honors.

The wounded head healed pictures the persecuting power surviving its apparent death and reviving in a new form; the names of the redeemed were written in the Lamb's book before the foundation of the world.
The beast composites Daniel's leopard, bear, and lion, and its 'was given' authority — mouth, war, and dominion alike — is real yet permitted, bounded by God at every turn.
Reads the beast as the kingdom of Antichrist gathered from many nations — its bear's feet its leaders, its lion's mouth a tongue bent on the shedding of blood.

external.name: Robert H. Mounce
external.point: The mortally wounded head that revives reflects the Nero redivivus legend alive in first-century Asia; the beast is Rome as persecuting empire, though the imagery finally transcends any single emperor into the recurring reality of idolatrous power.

1.application: The beast still rules by parody — borrowing the shapes of resurrection, universality, and worship to win an allegiance it cannot deserve. The chapter's answer to overwhelming power is not a strategy but a register: your name in the Lamb's book, and the endurance that flows from knowing the beast's forty-two months are already counted.

11.exposition: A second beast now rises — this one out of the earth, with two horns like a lamb but a dragon's voice. It is the propaganda arm of the first: it wields the first beast's authority and bends all its energy to making the world worship the beast whose mortal wound was healed. It works signs — even fire from heaven — to deceive, orders an image of the beast to be made, and gives the image breath to speak and to kill those who will not bow. Then it brands the world: a mark on every right hand or forehead, without which none may buy or sell. The chapter closes on a riddle for the wise — the number of the beast, the number of a man, 666. Later named 'the false prophet' (19:20), this beast completes an unholy trinity in which dragon, sea-beast, and land-beast counterfeit Father, Son, and Spirit.
11.original_language: The land-beast has 'two horns like a lamb' — arnion, the very word for the Lamb of ch. 5 — but it 'spoke like a dragon,' the disguise betrayed by the voice. Later chapters give its name: ho pseudoprophētēs, 'the false prophet' (16:13). The 'mark' is charagma, a stamp or brand — the word for an emperor's imprint on coins and documents, and for the tattoo-brand borne by slaves and soldiers. The closing number is set as gematria (Greek isopsēphia): since letters double as numerals, a name can be 'counted.' Arithmos anthrōpou, 'the number of a man,' may mean a number reckoned in the ordinary human way, or the number belonging to one particular man.
11.historical_context: In first-century Asia the 'false prophet' answers to the provincial machinery of the imperial cult — the priesthoods that built the temples, staged the festivals, and policed participation in the worship of Caesar. Calling fire down from heaven parodies Elijah (1 Kings 18:38; 2 Kings 1:10). The economic exclusion of v.17 was no abstraction: Christians who would not offer the trade-guild's ritual acknowledgment of the gods faced real loss of livelihood. As for the number, the strongest ancient computation is gematria for Nero Caesar: the Hebrew letters of Nrwn Qsr total 666, and the shorter spelling Nrw Qsr totals 616 — which neatly explains the ancient variant reading '616.' Irenaeus, not reckoning in Hebrew, proposed instead the Greek Lateinos (the Latin/Roman power), whose letters also total 666.
11.christ: The land-beast is the sharpest christological parody in the book: it apes the Lamb itself, wearing lamb's horns while speaking the dragon's words, and its work mimics the Spirit's — it gives 'breath' to a dead image and works signs to draw worship, as the Spirit gives life and testifies to Christ. Its mark on hand and forehead is the counterfeit of the seal of God: every person bears either the beast's mark or the Lamb's name on the forehead (14:1). And 666, 'the number of a man,' is fallen human sovereignty grasping at divine prerogative — six falling thrice short of the sevenfold perfection sung as 'Holy, holy, holy' — over against the Lamb, whose sovereignty is genuine because it is truly divine.

verses.note: Another beast comes up out of the earth, with two horns like a lamb, and speaks like a dragon. reformed Gill and grammatical-historical Barnes read a power religious rather than merely political — lamb-like in its show of piety, dragon-like in its speech; Gill: it 'had two horns like a lamb… but he spoke like a dragon.' It is later named 'the false prophet' (16:13; 19:20). eastern Cyril and the fathers see the deceiver who comes in sheep's clothing (Matthew 7:15). The crux persists: papal or ecclesiastical Rome to historicists, the end-time false prophet to futurists.

The second beast exercises all the authority of the first beast in its presence, and makes the earth worship the first beast whose mortal wound was healed. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that it acts wholly as the first beast's agent and promoter; its entire office is to direct the world's homage to the revived persecuting power (v.3) — religion pressed into the service of empire.

It works great signs, even making fire come down from heaven before men. grammatical-historical Barnes and reformed Gill call these the lying wonders that authenticate the deception (2 Thessalonians 2:9; Matthew 24:24) — whether real satanic marvels or imposture. The fire from heaven is a deliberate counterfeit of Elijah's sign (1 Kings 18:38), the false prophet mimicking the true.

By these signs it deceives the earth-dwellers, bidding them make an image to the beast that was wounded by the sword and yet lived. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear an echo of Nebuchadnezzar's golden image (Daniel 3): beast-worship is now given a visible object. The image honors the revived beast (v.3) — historicists read it of an institution or system imposed to enforce that worship.

The false prophet is given power to give breath to the image, so that it speaks and has those who will not worship it killed. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note how the animated, speaking image deepens the delusion, while the death-decree recalls the fiery furnace for those who would not bow (Daniel 3). Worship is now backed by lethal compulsion.

It causes all — small and great, rich and poor, free and slave — to receive a mark on the right hand or the forehead. grammatical-historical Barnes likens the charagma to the brand borne by slaves and soldiers or the token of a devotee, set on the most conspicuous parts. reformed Gill and Barnes see a studied parody of God's sealing of his servants (7:3) and of his word bound on hand and forehead (Deuteronomy 6:8): the mark is open, professed allegiance to the beast.

The purpose is coercion: no one may buy or sell without the mark, the name of the beast, or the number of its name. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott take the three as equivalent badges of submission, and the exclusion as economic strangulation — the faithful shut out of trade and livelihood. Persecution now works not only by the sword (v.15) but by want.

'Here is wisdom: let the one who has understanding calculate the number of the beast, for it is the number of a man, and his number is 666.' The number is a cryptogram to be reckoned by gematria, and interpreters divide honestly over the solution. grammatical-historical Barnes revived the ancient guess of Irenaeus — Lateinos (Λατεινος), the Latin/Roman power, whose Greek letters total 666. grammatical-historical Robertson weighs the preterist computation, 'Nero Caesar' (Hebrew Nrwn Qsr) = 666, with the variant spelling yielding 616 — the very alternate reading preserved in some manuscripts, which strengthens the Nero case. Many read the number symbolically: 'the number of a man' is humanity's six falling thrice short of the divine seven, the brand of a power supremely imperfect. Historicists have applied it to the papal line. The text itself invites sober counting, not sensational date-setting — a riddle for the wise, never a timetable.

witnesses.point: The land-beast is lamb-like in its outward profession of religion but speaks like a dragon — a show of piety masking the devil's own temper and design.
The mark is drawn from the branding of slaves and soldiers — a conspicuous, public token of ownership set on hand or forehead, a counterfeit of the seal of God.
666 is a cryptogram to be computed; the Hebrew letters of 'Neron Kaisar' total 666, and the shorter spelling 616, explaining the ancient textual variant.
The second beast is the antichristian deceiver who comes in sheep's clothing — gentle in appearance, satanic in speech.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Reads 666 as gematria the community was meant to decode, not a prediction: the Hebrew letters of 'Nero Caesar' (Nrwn Qsr) sum to 666 and the shorter spelling to 616 — the earliest variant — which together make the Nero solution the most defensible, while the triple sixes also brand the beast's persistent falling-short of perfection.

11.application: The most threatening element here is the quietest: not the fire from heaven but the ledger — the daily, commercial pressure to wear the beast's name in order to eat. Revelation does not promise the faithful an exemption from that cost; it promises that the mark on the hand decides nothing the name on the forehead (14:1) has not already decided. Allegiance is worn somewhere; wisdom is knowing whose you are.

_meta.title: The Two Beasts and the Mark of 666
reflection: The beast wins the world by parody — a slain-and-healed head, a mouth of great claims, an acclamation stolen from the praise of God (vv.3–4). Where are you tempted to be awed by a power that only imitates the crucified and risen Lamb?

'Here is a call for the endurance and faith of the saints' (v.10) is spoken to people the beast is permitted, for a time, to conquer. What changes if faithfulness is measured by endurance rather than by visible victory?

The mark decides who may buy and sell (vv.16–17), while the Lamb's book of life, written before the world began, decides who is truly his (v.8). Where does loyalty to Christ actually cost you something, and how does the book of life steady you there?
